extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin studied is a thermal incubator – an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heatbed and a fan for heat distribution, two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor, all controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The system’s objective is to achieve and maintain a target temperature inside the box regardless of its contents, making it a representative, low‑cost cyber‑physical system suitable for Digital Twin experimentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers real‑time monitoring of temperature measurements, model‑based simulation of the incubator thermal dynamics with two predictive models (Model A – 2‑parameter; Model B – 4‑parameter), and online parameter calibration using non‑linear least‑squares estimation. These services allow operators to observe the current state, validate the thermal model, and update the model parameters during operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation leverages a Raspberry Pi 4 controller for device interfacing, a low‑level driver that reads sensor data and pushes updates to a RabbitMQ message broker, and a controller that applies a bang‑bang strategy with configurable limits, heating duration, and waiting duration. The simulation models are formulated as differential equations (heat equation Q=cmΔT) and calibrated with a non‑linear least‑squares solver. All source code and datasets are hosted as an open‑source GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin exchanges information with the physical incubator through an event‑driven message bus (RabbitMQ).  The low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi periodically reads the two inside temperature sensors and the outside temperature sensor and publishes the readings to a RabbitMQ queue; the controller consumes these messages, drives the fan and heatbed, and publishes its control state back to the same queue.  The temporal granularity of the DT is thus defined by the polling interval of the low‑level driver and by the message round‑trip through RabbitMQ; the exact cycle time is not specified in the documents, but the system is designed to operate within a few seconds per observation cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT is implemented on a single Raspberry Pi platform and follows a centralized CPS‑centric architecture.  Key components include: 1) the low‑level driver, which abstracts sensor/actuator I/O with a safe protocol and publishes sensor data to RabbitMQ; 2) a RabbitMQ communication server hosted on the Pi, acting as the central message broker for bi‑directional data and control flow; 3) the controller, a bang‑bang temperature regulator with parameters LL, UL, H, and C, that consumes sensor topics and publishes control commands; 4) a real‑time dashboard that subscribes to broker topics, writes telemetry to a time‑series database, and presents live visualizations; 5) an on‑demand calibration service that invokes a non‑linear least‑squares solver to tune the parameters of Model B during experimental runs; 6) a Kalman filter module that uses the calibrated Model B for anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation; 7) a co‑simulation module for what‑if experiments that integrates historical or live PT data with the dynamic models.  All services communicate exclusively via RabbitMQ topics, enabling modular scaling while maintaining tight coupling for rapid feedback loops and supporting the self‑adaptation loop (detection → experiment → calibration → re‑control).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT implements two analytical behavioral models (Model A and Model B) that predict the temperature inside the insulated styrofoam incubator. Model A assumes 100 % conversion of electrical power to air heating and neglects heatbed thermal inertia. Model B relaxes this assumption and introduces a fourth free parameter to capture heatbed inertia, making it better suited for control. Both models are calibrated online via a calibration algorithm that estimates the free parameters from sensor data. The data feed is collected by two temperature sensors inside the box and a third outside sensor, which are polled repeatedly by a low‑level driver that uploads the samples to a RabbitMQ communication server. The DT consumes these messages, stores them in a time‑series database, and uses them for runtime monitoring, Kalman‑filter‑based state estimation, anomaly detection, and what‑if simulation. The models therefore support simulation, prediction, optimisation, and supervisory control within the DT framework.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuators are the heatbed and the fan inside the incubator. Both are controlled in an on/off fashion by a Raspberry Pi running a bang‑bang controller. The controller’s decision logic is parameterised by a lower temperature limit (LL), an upper limit (UL), a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C). Actuation commands are sent and received through messages published to and subscribed from a RabbitMQ communication server. A low‑level driver enforces a safe protocol that guarantees the actuators remain in a safe state and that temperature excursions remain within LL–UL bounds. The actuators are addressed by unique identifiers within the communication infrastructure and are remotely controllable via the DT software.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The incubator is equipped with three resistive temperature sensors: two placed inside the insulated box and one outside to capture ambient temperature. The sensors are sampled periodically by the low‑level driver; the exact sampling interval is not specified in the documents but the driver polls at a regular cadence and forwards the readings to RabbitMQ. Sensor data is serialized and transmitted over the message broker, then ingested by the DT. Accuracy and precision specifications are not provided, but the uniform‑temperature assumption is validated experimentally by comparing the two interior sensor readings. Spatial distribution is confined to the two interior points and the exterior point, with the internal sensors’ measurements assumed representative of the entire box due to fan‑mediated mixing.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is defined by the set of assumptions underlying Model A and Model B: Assumption 1 (uniform internal temperature), Assumption 2 (negligible wall heat accumulation), Assumption 3 (constant air specific heat), and Assumption 4 (complete conversion of electrical power to air heating). Model B removes Assumption 4, adding a parameter that captures the heatbed’s thermal inertia. Calibration is performed online by estimating the free parameters via a least‑squares or similar optimisation routine whenever new data are collected. Validation and verification rely on co‑simulation between the DT and the real incubator, what‑if simulation scenarios, and Kalman‑filter‑based anomaly detection that flags deviations from expected temperature trajectories. Uncertainty quantification is implicit in the Kalman filter’s covariance; error detection is realised through monitor violations of safety bounds and drift detection. Corrective actions include re‑calibration, controller re‑configuration, and controller parameter optimisation. Quality assurance rests on periodic sensor validation, safety‑protocol enforcement by the low‑level driver, and the ability to replay historical data for model‑dataset consistency checks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the physical twin are collected by two temperature sensors positioned inside the insulated styrofoam box and one temperature sensor measuring ambient air outside the box. Each sensor outputs a temperature value in degrees Celsius as a floating‑point number. The low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi periodically reads these sensor values (exact sampling frequency not specified in the documentation) and publishes them to the RabbitMQ message broker using the AMQP protocol. A safe protocol embedded in the driver ensures that only validated sensor readings are transmitted. The digital twin components listen to the RabbitMQ queue, aggregate the two interior sensor readings to an average, and store the resulting time‑series in a local database for subsequent visualization. No explicit data‑quality checks or preprocessing steps beyond the average calculation are described.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the digital twin controller running on the Raspberry Pi. The controller receives temperature measurements from RabbitMQ, executes a bang‑bang control strategy, and publishes command messages to RabbitMQ to turn the heatbed and fan on or off. Command messages are conveyed over AMQP using a simple payload that indicates actuator state (e.g., 'heatbed: ON', 'fan: OFF'). A safe protocol in the low‑level driver validates incoming commands, ensures they obey safety limits, and confirms execution by publishing status updates back to the broker. The low‑level driver then forwards the validated command to the corresponding actuator and reports execution success or failure. No authentication, encryption, or conflict resolution mechanisms beyond the safety checks are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical backbone of the incubator DT comprises an AMQP‑based RabbitMQ message broker that mediates all data and control traffic between the low‑level driver, controller, and the virtual twin components. The architecture is a star topology where the Raspberry Pi (hosting the driver and controller) connects to the local network and publishes/subscribes to RabbitMQ queues. The communication relies on the AMQP protocol; no explicit bandwidth or latency requirements are specified. RabbitMQ can be configured in a clustered setup to provide redundancy and high availability, although such configuration is not shown in the documentation. The implementation does not describe additional security protocols such as TLS or access control; all messages are exchanged on the trusted local network.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire Digital Twin stack is hosted on the Raspberry Pi linked to the incubator. The low‑level driver, the bang‑bang controller, the RabbitMQ server, and the digital twin services that subscribe to message feeds all run locally on the Pi (edge deployment). The system stores aggregated data and time‑series records on the Pi’s onboard storage, with the possibility to publish it to external dashboards. The deployment is managed on‑premises, aligning with the documented “on‑premises and in the cloud” capability of RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The incubator Digital Twin follows a four‑phase lifecycle. 1) **Design & Model Development** – Physical and cyber subsystems are described (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, temperaturesensors, Raspberry Pi controller) and two simulation models are built (Model A with four free parameters, Model B with two). 2) **Integration & Calibration** – The models are integrated into the DT framework (low‑level driver, RabbitMQ communication server, Kalman‑filter‑based monitor). During operation on‑demand calibration (Section 3.3) restores model parameters to the best‑fit values. 3) **Monitoring & Adaptation** – Continuous data streams from PT sensors feed the DT; monitors check safety and performance, triggering anomaly detection or controller re‑configuration. 4) **Experiment & Re‑calibration** – When a change in the plant is detected (e.g., a new bucket of water), the DT schedules experiments, gathers new data, recalibrates Model B, and re‑optimises the controller. Transition between stages is managed by the calibration loop that shifts the twin from predictive simulation to monitoring mode and back. No explicit lifecycle governance or management pattern is documented in the provided materials.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Twinning process is implemented using a combination of established CPS engineering practices: model‑based design (Deterministic differential‑equation models for thermal dynamics), simulation (co‑simulation of MT and PT states), calibration (on‑demand optimisation of Model B parameters), and control (bang‑bang controller with statechart and safety timings).  Repository management is performed via GitHub repositories (see links in Documents 2 and 5) providing version control for source code, models and calibration scripts.  Testing and validation are performed by comparing simulation outputs against measured temperature traces; Model B consistently outperforms Model A in predicting thermal inertia.  Evolutionary strategies include iterative model refinement, on‑demand calibration, and self‑adaptation loops that re‑configure the controller and re‑optimise it via what‑if simulations.  Quality assurance follows the standard software development lifecycle of code review, unit testing of driver and controller modules, and end‑to‑end integration testing of the RabbitMQ communication flow.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is provided through: 1) **Anomaly Detection & Heat‑bed State Estimation** – A Kalman filter that fuses Model B predictions with dual temperature sensors supplies a real‑time estimate of the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and flags out‑of‑range behaviour. 2) **Real‑time Visualization** – Sensor data and controller status are streamed to a time‑series database and plotted on a dashboard for operator monitoring. 3) **What‑if Simulations** – Co‑simulation of historical or live PT data allows optimisation of controller parameters (e.g., heating duration H, waiting duration C) before deployment.  4) **Self‑Adaptation Loop** – The DT automatically detects behavioural change, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and finally re‑optimises the controller, providing confidence‑aware recommendations via the Kalman filter covariance.  Confidence measures are implicit in the Kalman filter covariance and in the quality of fit between simulation and measurement.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol to keep the incubator in a safe operating state.  This safety‑enhancing mechanism ensures that sensor readings and actuator commands are processed only when the system is confirmed to be within safe limits, thereby preventing hazardous operating conditions.  The documents do not specify additional security or access‑control measures beyond this safe‑protocol implementation.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
